text.title: AI Research Tool Stack
text.summary: Stage-to-tool mapping for a structured AI research pipeline with workflow steps and best-practice rules.

section.title: Tool Stack by Stage
section.p: Assign exactly one primary tool per research stage. Using the same tool across stages hides coverage gaps and degrades source attribution.

rule.statement: Map each research stage to its specialist tool before starting any query.
rule.rationale: Perplexity excels at cited multi-source synthesis; competitor intelligence tools (Crayon, Klue) are optimized for tracking product and pricing changes; interview analysis tools (Looppanel, Dovetail) require pre-uploaded transcripts. A general-purpose chat model cannot substitute any of these at scale.

list.item: Deep Research — Perplexity Pro Search, Claude Research Mode
Competitor Analysis — Crayon, Klue, Kompyte, Contify, AlphaSense
Market Data — Statista AI, CB Insights
Trend Analysis — Exploding Topics, Google Trends
Interview Analysis — Looppanel, Dovetail, Insight7, UserBit

rule.statement: Execute research stages sequentially: define questions → broad AI exploration → human curation → AI synthesis → human analysis → AI report draft → human finalization.
rule.rationale: A human checkpoint between broad exploration and deep research prevents propagating low-quality seeds downstream. Skipping curation compounds hallucination risk in the synthesis step.

rule.statement: Document which AI tool produced each finding in the final report.
rule.rationale: Reviewers need source attribution to calibrate trust. Hiding AI involvement or omitting tool names prevents reproducibility and trust auditing.

rule.statement: Use Haiku for mechanical tool invocation and parameter setup; Sonnet for multi-source synthesis; Opus for validating critical data points where hallucination risk is high.
rule.rationale: Model cost scales with complexity. Mechanical dispatch does not require frontier reasoning; synthesis does. Opus is reserved for high-stakes validation, not routine queries.

rule.statement: Verify AI findings against primary sources before including in deliverables.
rule.rationale: 95% of researchers use AI tools regularly (2025), but citation quality varies: Perplexity cites URLs, Claude/ChatGPT can hallucinate references. Primary-source verification is the mandatory last step.

antipattern.description: Using one general-purpose chat model for all research stages.
antipattern.reason: Interview-analysis, competitor-intel, and market-data stages each require specialist tooling; a single LLM cannot substitute all three and produces shallow, biased, citation-poor results.

antipattern.description: Trusting AI output without human analysis.
antipattern.reason: The era of "best AI assistant" as a single winner is over (2026). The right assistant is task-matched; no tool triangulates all stages autonomously — human curation between stages is mandatory.
